retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Tumor (Osteoclastoma) -- Evaluation -- Imaging Modalities
retrieved_docs.content: MRI is a crucial tool for assessing the integrity of the surrounding soft tissues, neurovascular structures, and the extent of subchondral extension into adjacent joints. GCT lesions typically present with homogeneous signal intensity, appearing as well-circumscribed lesions on MRI. On T1-weighted images, these lesions exhibit low signal intensity, while on T2-weighted images, intermediate signal intensity is observed. The typical features include an expansile hypervascular mass with cystic changes, displaying heterogeneous low to intermediate signal intensity on T1-weighted images and intermediate to high intensity on T2-weighted images. [24] [25] Notably, areas of low signal intensity on both T1 and T2-weighted images are attributed to the accumulation of significant amounts of hemosiderin. [20] MRI helps assess the soft tissue mass and cystic components within the tumor. For the evaluation of residual or recurrent GCT, fat-suppression fluid-sensitive sequences in MRI are useful. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Central (Intramedullary): [6] High-Grade Central Conventional – “classic” appearance; cells are spindle-like to polyhedral in shape; nuclei are variable in appearance, and cells undergoing mitosis are readily identifiable. Matrix production by the tumor cells may be osseous (“osteoblastic”), cartilaginous (“chondroblastic”), or fibrous (“fibroblastic”) but a combination of the three often presents. Osteoid matrix must be identified somewhere in the lesion, even if only in a minuscule amount. In the case of osteoblastic osteosarcoma, there may be excessive osteoid matrix production such that the tumor is described as “sclerosing” in appearance. Osteoblastoma-like – histologically resembles osteoblastoma but features more cellular atypia and local aggressiveness, and osteoid matrix production Chondroblastoma-like – histological resembles chondroblastoma but features more cellular atypia, local aggressiveness, and osteoid matrix production Chondromyxoid fibroma-like – histological resembles chondromyxoid fibroma but features more cellular atypia, local aggressiveness, and osteoid matrix production Malignant fibrous histiocytoma-like – histologically resembles malignant fibrous histiocytoma but features more cellular atypia, local aggressiveness, and osteoid matrix production Epithelioid – features cells that are so poorly differentiated that it may be hard to distinguish histologically whether the lesion is a sarcoma (connective tissue origin) or a carcinoma (epithelial origin) Giant cell – features benign multinucleated osteoclast-like giant cells Clear cell – features numerous cells with clear or ground-glass cytoplasm and vacuoles Telangiectatic – comprised of numerous blood-filled sinusoids such that the tumor mimics the histology of an aneurysmal bone cyst; the presence of pleomorphic/atypical nuclei will distinguish the malignancy. Small Cell – considered to be a histological combination of Ewing sarcoma and osteosarcoma, features numerous small round cells. A minuscule volume of an osteoid matrix will distinguish this as a variant of osteosarcoma. Low-Grade Central Fibrous dysplasia–like – features a large volume of osseous matrix embedded in a small amount of fibrous stroma. Desmoplastic fibroma–like – features a very small volume of osseous matrix embedded in a large amount of fibrous stroma.

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Tumor (Osteoclastoma) -- Evaluation -- Imaging Modalities
retrieved_docs.content: Radiograph examination typically reveals a characteristic radiolucent geographic appearance with a narrow transition zone at the lesion margin (see Image. Lytic Lesion). Unlike many benign lesions, GCT lacks a prominent sclerotic rim at the lesion margin. Calcification of the matrix, periosteal reaction, and new bone formation are typically absent. [20] The lesion is eccentrically located in the epiphyseal portion and tends to extend up to a centimeter into the subchondral bone.

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Tumor (Osteoclastoma) -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In benign bone GCTs, 3 distinct cell types are identified: [26] Type I cells: Resembling interstitial fibroblasts; these cells produce collagen and exhibit proliferative capabilities. This cell likely constitutes the tumor component of GCT and shares features with mesenchymal stem cells. Their characteristics suggest a potential early differentiation into osteoblasts. [27] Type II cells: Also interstitial, these cells resemble the monocyte/macrophage family and could potentially be recruited from the peripheral bloodstream. [1] Type II cells serve as precursors to the multinucleated giant cells within the tumor. Type III cells: Represented by multinucleated giant cells, these cells share many characteristics with osteoclasts and display similar morphologies. [15] Type III cells possess enzymes for bone resorption, including tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase and type II carbonic anhydrase. [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Aneurysmal Bone Cysts -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Giant cell tumor (GCT) – GCT is a nonmalignant tumor of the bone that typically presents in adults who have reached skeletal maturity. They are aggressive osteolytic neoplasms that may have distant metastases to the lungs; these metastases are usually benign. Although the majority are nonmalignant, up to 8% of GCTs will undergo malignant transformation. [12] GCT commonly presents as chronic pain and swelling at the site, with limitation of movement of the nearby joint. If weight-bearing bones are involved, a pathologic fracture may occur. Radiographs will show a lesion, typically in the epiphysis, with a non-central lytic focus. Subchondral plate involvement is not uncommon. CT will show profound cortical thinning and reveal the absence of matrix mineralization. GCT is the most commonly involved tumor seen with secondary aneurysmal bone cysts. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Tumor (Osteoclastoma) -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Type II and type III cells exhibit significant activity for insulin-like growth factors 1 and 2, while this activity is notably absent in type I cells. This observation implies that insulin-like growth factors 1 and 2 play a crucial role in the development and regulation of GCTs. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Differential Diagnosis -- Benign Types of Tumors
retrieved_docs.content: Giant cell tumor Osteoblastoma Enchondroma Chondromyxoid fibroma Cortical desmoid

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Tumor (Osteoclastoma) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The giant cells carry out the primary task of tumor bone resorption within the tumor. The spindle-like stromal cells are pivotal in recruiting monocytes and facilitating their fusion into giant cells. The stromal cells also improve the resorptive capabilities of the giant cells, contributing to the overall bone resorption mechanism.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Odontogenic tumors that arise from ectomesenchyme with or without included odontogenic epithelium:
retrieved_docs.content: Lastly, cementoblastoma resembles osteoblastoma and osteoid osteoma, except that it is usually in continuity with the cemental layer of the roots. Eosinophilic matrix rimmed by cementoblast cells. It is relatively acellular in the periphery with a more vascular and mineralized center. The center usually shows multinucleated giant cells with reversal lines which indicates extensive remodeling. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Introduction -- Histocyte Derived
retrieved_docs.content: Benign Central giant cell granulomas Malignant Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Tumor (Osteoclastoma) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate between giant cell tumor and other bone lesions through a comprehensive analysis of histological and imaging findings. Implement evidence-based diagnostic and therapeutic interventions for giant cell tumor, considering the tumor's location and potential for recurrence. Apply updated knowledge of molecular markers, such as H3F3A mutations, to enhance diagnostic accuracy and guide treatment decisions. Collaborate with an interprofessional team, including orthopedic surgeons, radiologists, and pathologists, to ensure comprehensive and coordinated care for giant cell tumor patients. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Tumor (Osteoclastoma) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mutations in the H3F3A gene, found in over 90% of GCT, are implicated in driving tumorigenesis. These mutations are restricted to the stromal cell population and are absent in osteoclasts or their precursors. [19] Neoplastic stromal cells likely possess an immature osteoblast phenotype, expressing markers such as RANKL and other indicators of the early osteoblast lineage within their transcriptional repertoire. [20] The activation of stromal cells has been hypothesized to occur not from inherent genetic changes but rather from the local release of red cells and plasma proteins into the matrix induced by hemorrhage. Maintaining the stromal cells' immature state may involve unknown reciprocal signals from giant cells. RANKL has been identified as a primary molecular target for therapeutic interventions. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Tumor (Osteoclastoma) -- Evaluation -- Biopsy and Immunohistochemistry
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy samples undergo immunohistochemistry, and identifying the H3.3-G34W mutation is sensitive and specific for diagnosing GCT, helping differentiate it from other giant cell-rich tumors. [46] [47] Furthermore, detecting this mutation provides valuable insights into the molecular characteristics of GCT, informing potential therapeutic approaches and prognostic considerations for patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Tumor (Osteoclastoma) -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Upon gross inspection, these lesions exhibit characteristic features such as chocolate brown, soft and spongy texture, and fragility. [24] Yellow-to-orange discoloration from the hemosiderin can also be present. Commonly, cystic blood-filled cavities within the tumor may be observed. [1] Examination typically reveals a variable degree of cortical expansion and disruption while the periosteum remains intact. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Tumor (Osteoclastoma) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Several studies identified a high expression of RANKL by stromal cells within GCTs. [15] [16] These stromal cells also secrete factors that can regulate or prevent osteoclastogenesis, including osteoprotegerin, serving as a natural negative regulator of RANKLE that obstructs osteoclast and osteoblast interactions and functions as a natural negative regulator of RANKL. [17] The expression of RANKL by the osteoblast-like mononuclear stromal cells stimulates the recruitment of the osteoclastic cells from a normal monocytic pre-osteoclast cell. The osteoclastic giant cells then actively absorb host bone via a cathepsin K and matrix metalloproteinase 13-mediated process, which would account for the osteolysis associated with these tumors. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Tumor (Osteoclastoma) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation for GCT involves a combination of laboratory and imaging studies, followed by a biopsy for a definitive diagnosis. Below is a detailed discussion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is identified as a pathological finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of infiltrating malignant cells within the affected bone tissue. Likewise, Tumor cells, malignant are recognized as the abnormal cellular component that characterizes سرطانة العظم, reflecting the disease’s neoplastic nature. Together, these findings underscore that both the infiltrative pattern of malignant cells and the presence of malignant tumor cells are key diagnostic features of سرطانة العظم, aiding clinicians in confirming the diagnosis and guiding appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2564
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that this malignant condition involves the upper jaw and reflects a neoplastic process affecting both bone and connective tissue structures in that region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding that indicates the presence of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The detection of malignant cellular infiltrate in tissue samples signals that cancerous cells have invaded the bone, serving as an important diagnostic marker for confirming bone malignancy and guiding subsequent therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is a malignant condition that involves the skeletal system and its supporting connective tissues. It has a broader relationship with ورم العظام, indicating that bone tumors (ورم العظام) are encompassed within this wider category of cancer. Recognizing that ورم العظام represents a specific manifestation of cancer of bone and connective tissue aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, appropriate classification, and targeted therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cells that define the disease known as سرطانة العظم (bone cancer).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2716
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are identified as the abnormal cells that underlie the disease entity known as bone tumor (ورم العظام).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2655
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of bone cancer. The identification of a malignant cellular infiltrate reflects the invasion of cancerous cells into bone tissue, while a secondary lesion denotes additional disease sites that arise as the primary bone tumor progresses. Recognizing these findings together aids clinicians in confirming the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم, assessing disease extent, and guiding appropriate therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2513
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Metastatic Lesion are both recognized as pathological findings of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The presence of a malignant cellular infiltrate indicates that cancerous cells have invaded the bone tissue, while a metastatic lesion reflects the spread of tumor cells to bone from another primary site. Identifying either of these findings on imaging or histopathology supports the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم and guides clinicians in staging the disease, planning appropriate treatment, and monitoring for disease progression.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2192
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face is a specific type of neoplasm of bone of the face, representing a malignant tumor that originates in the facial bones. This relationship indicates that the broader category “neoplasm of bone of the face” encompasses the more specific entity “primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face,” clarifying the hierarchical classification of facial bone tumors for diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: trauma, surgery, age‑related degeneration, calcific deposition, congenital malformations, and metastatic cancer each generate distinct but often overlapping structural and functional disturbances in the posterior segment, necessitating comprehensive ocular assessment and targeted management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 240
retrieved_docs.content: similarly defined as a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation. In addition, an inverse‑is‑a relationship links the entity “سرطانة توتية” to the primary malignant tumor morphology (“ورم خبيث أولي”), further emphasizing its classification as a type of primary malignant neoplasm. Together, these relationships illustrate how various primary malignant neoplasms across different organs are unified by common pathological processes, shared morphological descriptors, and hierarchical links to broader cancer concepts, aiding in consistent classification and clinical understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1126
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is linked to the broader Arabic concept “ورم العظام” (bone tumor) and is specifically mapped to “ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي” (malignant tumor of the upper jaw). In addition, the Arabic term “سرطان العظم” (bone cancer) is classified as a malignant tumor of the upper jaw. Together, these relationships indicate that cancer of bone and connective tissue is understood as a type of malignant upper‑jaw tumor, connecting the general notion of a bone tumor with a precise anatomical manifestation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2168
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary Lesion is identified as a finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the presence of secondary lesions may support the diagnosis of this bone malignancy. Conversely, Tumor cells, benign are not considered a finding of سرطانة العظم, meaning that benign tumor cells do not contribute to the identification or confirmation of this disease.
